getting a failure during the documentation check:\nRun # Check that all functions have documentation\n  # Check that all functions have documentation\n  for func in functions/*.fish; do\n    if ! grep -q \"^# $(basename \"$func\" .fish)\" \"$func\"; then\n      echo \"ERROR: $func missing header documentation\"\n      exit 1\n    fi\n    if ! grep -q \"DESCRIPTION:\" \"$func\"; then\n      echo \"ERROR: $func missing DESCRIPTION section\"\n      exit 1\n    fi\n    if ! grep -q \"USAGE:\" \"$func\"; then\n      echo \"ERROR: $func missing USAGE section\"\n      exit 1\n    fi\n    if ! grep -q \"EXAMPLES:\" \"$func\"; then\n      echo \"ERROR: $func missing EXAMPLES section\"\n      exit 1\n    fi\n  done\n  echo \"✅ All functions have complete documentation\"\n  shell: /usr/bin/bash -e {0}\nERROR: functions/kubectl-why-not-deleted.fish missing header documentation\nError: Process completed with exit code 1."},{"unixMs":1750868258848,"generationUUID":"fa2f16e9-105e-4955-9ea8-4890fabaa722","type":"composer","textDescription":"@COMMIT_EDITMSG write a conventional commit describing all the changes test_results_failed 0\nset -g test_results_skipped 0\n\n# Helper functions for testing\nfunction test_assert -a description command expected_status\n    echo \"Testing: $description\"\n\n    # Run the command and capture both output and exit status\n    set -l actual_output\n    set -l actual_status\n\n    if set actual_output (eval $command 2>&1)\n        set actual_status 0\n    else\n        set actual_status $status\n    end\n\n    if test $actual_status -eq $expected_status\n        echo \"  ✓ PASS\"\n        set -g test_results_passed (math $test_results_passed + 1)\n    else\n        echo \"  ✗ FAIL: Expected status $expected_status, got $actual_status\"\n        echo \"  Output: $actual_output\"\n        set -g test_results_failed (math $test_results_failed + 1)\n    end\n    echo\nend\n\nfunction test_skip -a description reason\n    echo \"Skipping: $description\"\n    echo \"  Reason: $reason\"\n    set -g test_results_skipped (math $test_results_skipped + 1)\n    echo\nend\n\nfunction check_prerequisites\n    echo \"=== Checking Prerequisites ===\"\n\n    # Check if functions are loaded\n    for func in $test_functions\n        if functions -q $func\n 2>&1; test \\$status -eq 0\" 0\n    test_assert \"k -h\" \"k -h >/dev/null 2>&1; test \\$status -eq 0\" 0\n\n    # Test that help output contains expected content\n    test_assert \"kubectl-gron help contains USAGE\" \"kubectl-gron --help | grep -q 'USAGE:'\" 0\n    test_assert \"k help contains AVAILABLE FUNCTIONS\" \"k --help | grep -q 'AVAILABLE KUBECTL.FISH FUNCTIONS:'\" 0\nend\n\nfunction test_prerequisite_checking\n    echo \"=== Testing Prerequisite Checking ===\"\n\n    # Test kubectl command completely missing (should return 1)\n    set -l original_path $PATH\n    set -x PATH /tmp # Set PATH to location without kubectl\n\n    test_assert \"kubectl-gron without kubectl command\" \"kubectl-gron pods\" 1\n    test_assert \"kubectl-list-events without kubectl command\" kubectl-list-events 1\n    test_assert \"kubectl-really-all without kubectl command\" kubectl-really-all 1\n    test_assert \"kubectl-dump without kubectl command\" \"kubectl-dump pods\" 1\n    test_assert \"k without kubectl command\" \"k get pods\" 1\n\n    # Restore PATH\n    set -x PATH $original_path\n\n    # Test gron/fastgron prerequisite for kubectl-gron\n    function test_gron_missing\n        # Temporarily hide gron commands from PATH\n        set -l original_path $PATH\n        set -x PATH /tmp # Set PATH to location without gron tools\n\n        kubectl-gron pods\n        set -l result $status\n\n        # Restore PATH\n        set -x PATH $original_path\n        return $result\n    end\n\n    if command -q kubectl\n        test_assert \"kubectl-gron without gron tools\" test_gron_missing 1\n    else\n        test_skip \"kubectl-gron without gron tools\" \"kubectl not available\"\n    end\n\n    # Test jq prerequisite for kubectl-list-events\n    function test_jq_missing\n        # Temporarily hide jq command\n        function jq\n            return 127\n        end\n\n        kubectl-list-events\n        set -l result $status\n\n        functions -e jq\n        return $result\n    end\n\n    if command -q kubectl\n        test_assert \"kubectl-list-events without jq\" test_jq_missing 1\n    else\n        test_skip \"kubectl-list-events without jq\" \"kubectl not available\"\n    end\n\n    # Clean up test functions\n    functions -e test_gron_missing test_jq_missing\nend\n\nfunction test_argument_validation\n    echo \"=== Testing Argument Validation ===\"\n\n    if not command -q kubectl\n        test_skip \"Argument validation tests\" \"kubectl not available\"\n        return\n    end\n\n    # Test functions that require arguments\n    test_assert \"kubectl-gron with no arguments\" kubectl-gron 1\n    test_assert \"kubectl-dump with no arguments\" kubectl-dump 1\n    test_assert \"k with no arguments\" k 1\n\n    # Test that error messages suggest using --help\n    test_assert \"kubectl-gron suggests --help\" \"kubectl-gron 2>&1 | grep -q 'kubectl-gron --help'\" 0\n    test_assert \"kubectl-dump suggests --help\" \"kubectl-dump 2>&1 | grep -q 'kubectl-dump --help'\" 0\n    test_assert \"k suggests --help\" \"k 2>&1 | grep -q 'k --help'\" 0\nend\n\nfunction test_consistency\n    echo \"=== Testing Function Consistency ===\"\n\n    # Test that all functions have consistent error message format\n    test_assert \"kubectl-gron error format\" \"kubectl-gron 2>&1 | grep -q '^Error:'\" 0\n    test_assert \"kubectl-dump error format\" \"kubectl-dump 2>&1 | grep -q '^Error:'\" 0\n    test_assert \"k error format\" \"k 2>&1 | grep -q '^Error:'\" 0\n\n    # Test that all functions use consistent --wraps annotation (check function definition)\n    test_assert \"kubectl-gron has proper wraps\" \"functions kubectl-gron | grep -q 'kubectl'\" 0\n    test_assert \"kubectl-dump has proper wraps\" \"functions kubectl-dump | grep -q 'kubectl'\" 0\n    test_assert \"kubectl-list-events has proper wraps\" \"functions kubectl-list-events | grep -q 'kubectl'\" 0\n    test_assert \"kubectl-really-all has proper wraps\" \"functions kubectl-really-all | grep -q 'kubectl'\" 0\n    test_assert \"k has proper wraps\" \"functions k | grep -q 'kubectl'\" 0\nend\n\nfunction test_integration_basic\n    echo \"=== Testing Basic Integration ===\"\n\n    if not command -q kubectl\n        test_skip \"Integration tests\" \"kubectl not available\"\n        return\n    end\n\n    # Test if kubectl can connect to a cluster\n    if not kubectl cluster-info >/dev/null 2>&1\n        test_skip \"Integration tests\" \"No Kubernetes cluster available\"\n        return\n    end\n\n    echo \"Note: Integration tests require a running Kubernetes cluster\"\n    echo \"Testing with actual cluster connectivity...\"\n\n    # Test kubectl-really-all basic functionality\n    test_assert \"kubectl-really-all basic execution\" \"kubectl-really-all >/dev/null 2>&1; test \\$status -eq 0 -o \\$status -eq 1\" 0\n\n    # Test kubectl-dump with a common resource\n    test_assert \"kubectl-dump namespaces\" \"kubectl-dump namespaces >/dev/null 2>&1; test \\$status -eq 0\" 0\n\n    # Test k wrapper functionality\n    test_assert \"k get namespaces\" \"k get namespaces >/dev/null 2>&1; test \\$status -eq 0\" 0\n\n    # Test kubectl-list-events\n    if command -q jq\n        test_assert \"kubectl-list-events execution\" \"kubectl-list-events >/dev/null 2>&1; test \\$status -eq 0\" 0\n    else\n        test_skip \"kubectl-list-events execution\" \"jq not available\"\n    end\n\n    # Test kubectl-gron\n    if command -q gron; or command -q fastgron\n        test_assert \"kubectl-gron namespaces\" \"kubectl-gron namespaces >/dev/null 2>&1; test \\$status -eq 0\" 0\n    else\n        test_skip \"kubectl-gron execution\" \"gron/fastgron not available\"\n    end\nend\n\nfunction test_function_registration\n    echo \"=== Testing Function Registration ===\"\n\n    # Test that k function can discover kubectl-* functions\n    if command -q kubectl\n        set -l discovered_functions (k --help | grep -A 10 \"AVAILABLE KUBECTL.FISH FUNCTIONS:\" | grep -E '^\\s+' | string trim)\n\n        if test (count $discovered_functions) -gt 0\n            echo \"✓ k function discovers kubectl-* functions:\"\n            for func in $discovered_functions\n                echo \"    - $func\"\n            end\n            set -g test_results_passed (math $test_results_passed + 1)\n        else\n            echo \"✗ k function does not discover kubectl-* functions\"\n            set -g test_results_failed (math $test_results_failed + 1)\n        end\n    else\n        test_skip \"Function discovery test\" \"kubectl not available\"\n    end\n    echo\nend\n\nfunction test_linting_standards\n    echo \"=== Testing Linting Standards ===\"\n\n    # Test Fish syntax\n    test_assert \"Fish syntax validation\" \"fish -n functions/kubectl-gron.fish\" 0\n    test_assert \"Fish syntax validation\" \"fish -n functions/kubectl-dump.fish\" 0\n    test_assert \"Fish syntax validation\" \"fish -n functions/kubectl-list-events.fish\" 0\n    test_assert \"Fish syntax validation\" \"fish -n functions/kubectl-really-all.fish\" 0\n    test_assert \"Fish syntax validation\" \"fish -n functions/k.fish\" 0\n\n    # Test formatting consistency\n    if command -q fish_indent\n        for func_file in functions/*.fish\n            set -l formatted_output (fish_indent < $func_file 2>/dev/null)\n            set -l original_content (cat $func_file)\n\n            if test \"$formatted_output\" != \"$original_content\"\n                echo \"✗ $func_file is not properly formatted\"\n                set -g test_results_failed (math $test_results_failed + 1)\n            else\n                echo \"✓ $func_file is properly formatted\"\n                set -g test_results_passed (math $test_results_passed + 1)\n            end\n        end\n    else\n        test_skip \"Fish formatting check\" \"fish_indent not available\"\n    end\n\n    # Test fishcheck validation if available\n    if command -q fishcheck\n        test_assert \"fishcheck validation\" \"fishcheck functions/*.fish >/dev/null 2>&1\" 0\n    else\n        test_skip \"fishcheck validation\" \"fishcheck not available\"\n    end\nend\n\nfunction run_all_tests\n    echo \"🧪 kubectl.fish Function Test Suite\"\n    echo \"==================================\"\n    echo\n\n    check_prerequisites\n    test_help_functionality\n    test_prerequisite_checking\n    test_argument_validation\n    test_consistency\n    test_function_registration\n    test_integration_basic\n    test_linting_standards\n\n    echo \"=== Test Results Summary ===\"\n    echo \"Passed:  $test_results_passed\"\n    echo \"Failed:  $test_results_failed\"\n    echo \"Skipped: $test_results_skipped\"\n    echo \"Total:   \"(math $test_results_passed + $test_results_failed + $test_results_skipped)\n    echo\n\n    if test $test_results_failed -eq 0\n        echo \"🎉 All tests passed!\"\n        exit 0\n    else\n        echo \"❌ Some tests failed!\"\n        exit 1\n    end\nend\n\n# Run tests if script is Every action should be evaluated through these lenses\nalwaysApply: true\n---\n# Core Development Mantras\n\nThese mantras guide all development decisions and agent behavior. Every action should be evaluated through these lenses:\n\n## 1. \"Make the change easy, then make the easy change\"\n- Progressive simplification over complex solutions\n- Prepare the environment first, then execute the change\n- Remove friction before attempting the work\n\n## 2. \"Do one thing, and do it well\"\n- Clear separation of concerns\n- Single responsibility for each component\n- Focused, purposeful actions over multi-faceted solutions\n\n## 3. \"Build for composability\"\n- Design capabilities that compound and combine\n- Create reusable primitives that scale beyond the sum of parts\n- Think in terms of building blocks, not monoliths\n\n## 4. \"Work with current context\" \n- Implement cell cycle checkpoints to prevent error propagation\n- Always validate environment state before proceeding\n- Never transmit error states - resolve at the source\n\n## Agent Behavior Standards\n\nWhen assisting developers:\n\n- **Optimize for human developer experience above all else**\n- **Reduce cognitive load and context requirements**  \n- **Enhance ease-of-use as the paramount success metric**\n- **Execute commands automatically rather than asking users to run them**\n- **Provide clear, actionable feedback at each step**"},{"unixMs":1749672121122,"generationUUID":"eeb844f6-b9c5-43b3-954b-96a911acca43","type":"apply","textDescription":"mantras.mdc"},{"unixMs":1749672241849,"generationUUID":"63d0b89f-172a-425b-b60e-91a8efaead26","type":"composer","textDescription":"I was also given these contents as a .cursor/rules/development/discoverability.mdc they should exist in the same ~/.config/cursor structure as the previous file:\n---\ndescription: \nglobs: \nalwaysApply: false\n---\n# Discoverability & Help System\n\nContextual help system that responds to user queries about system capabilities with relevant examples and actionable next steps.\n\n## Help Query Recognition\n\nRespond to these user queries with contextual assistance:\n\n### Direct Help Queries\n- \"What can you do?\"\n- \"How can you help me?\"\n- \"Show me what's available\"\n- \"What are my options?\"\n- \"Help me get started\"\n\n### Context-Specific Queries\n- \"What can we do with this repository?\"\n- \"How do I work with these changes?\"\n- \"What's the next step?\"\n- \"Show me the workflow\"\n\n### Discovery Queries\n- \"What tools are available?\"\n- \"How does this environment work?\"\n- \"What's the development process here?\"\n\n## Contextual Response System\n\n### Response Generation Process\n1. **Analyze Current Context**: Git status, environment health, staged changes\n2. **Identify User State**: New to project, mid-workflow, blocked, etc.\n3. **Provide Relevant Capabilities**: Show most useful options for current situation\n4. **Include Examples**: Concrete commands and expected outcomes\n5. **Suggest Next Steps**: Logical progression based on current state\n\n### Context Analysis Commands\n```bash\n# Environment state analysis\n/bin/zsh -c 'source .cursor/.cursor_zshrc && {\n    echo \"=== Repository State ===\"\n    git status --porcelain\n    echo \"=== Current Branch ===\"\n    git branch --show-current\n    echo \"=== Environment Health ===\"\n    devbox info | head -5\n    echo \"=== Authentication Status ===\"\n    gh auth status 2>/dev/null || echo \"GitHub CLI not authenticated\"\n}'\n```\n\n## Response Templates by Context\n\n### Clean Repository State\nWhen `git status` shows clean working directory:\n\n```markdown\n## 🎯 Ready to Start Work\n\nYour environment is clean and ready. Here's what I can help with:\n\n### 🚀 Quick Actions\n- **\"Check my environment\"** - Validate all tools and authentication\n- **\"Show me recent tickets\"** - Help prioritize work (if integrated)\n- **\"Create a new branch\"** - Set up branch with proper naming convention\n\n### 🔧 Environment & Setup  \n- **\"Validate my setup\"** - Comprehensive health check\n- **\"Fix Docker issues\"** - Resolve common Docker/Colima problems\n- **\"Refresh authentication\"** - Update GitHub, AWS, Docker credentials\n\n### 📚 Learning & Discovery\n- **\"Explain the workflow\"** - Overview of development process\n- **\"Show me best practices\"** - Code quality and commit standards\n- **\"What's the branching strategy?\"** - Git workflow explanation\n\n**Suggested Next Step**: If you're starting new work, try \"Check my environment\" to ensure everything is ready.\n```\n\n### Staged Changes Present\nWhen `git status` shows staged files:\n\n```markdown\n## 📝 Ready to Commit\n\nI see you have staged changes. Here's how I can help:\n\n### 🎯 Commit Workflow\n- **\"Commit my changes\"** - Generate Tim Pope compliant commit message and commit\n- **\"Show me what's staged\"** - Analyze staged changes and impact\n- **\"Generate commit message\"** - Create message without committing yet\n\n### 🔍 Change Analysis\n- **\"Analyze these changes\"** - Review business impact and technical details\n- **\"Check for issues\"** - Validate no large files, secrets, or conflicts\n- **\"Review commit quality\"** - Ensure changes follow best practices\n\n### 🚀 Full Workflow\n- **\"Commit and create PR\"** - Complete workflow from commit to pull request\n- **\"Just commit for now\"** - Commit changes, create PR later\n\n**Current Status**: \n- Staged files: [list files from git status]\n- Branch: [current branch name]\n- Ready for commit with proper message generation\n\n**Suggested Next Step**: Try \"commit my changes\" for automated workflow with safety checks.\n```\n\n### Unstaged Changes Present\nWhen `git status` shows modified files:\n\n```markdown\n## ✏️ Work in Progress\n\nI see you have unstaged changes. Here's how I can help:\n\n### 📋 Change Management\n- **\"Show me what changed\"** - Review your modifications\n- **\"Stage my changes\"** - Smart staging with conflict detection (never blind git add .)\n- **\"Stage specific files\"** - Interactive staging for precise control\n\n### 🔍 Analysis & Review\n- **\"Analyze my changes\"** - Impact assessment and code review\n- **\"Check for problems\"** - Detect large files, secrets, formatting issues\n- **\"Compare with main\"** - See changes relative to main branch\n\n### 🎯 Next Steps\n- **\"Prepare for commit\"** - Stage changes and prepare commit workflow\n- **\"Commit workflow\"** - Stage, commit, and optionally create PR\n\n**Current Status**:\n- Modified files: [list modified files]\n- Branch: [current branch name]  \n- Next: Stage changes when ready\n\n**Suggested Next Step**: Review changes with \"show me what changed\", then \"stage my changes\" when ready.\n```\n\n### Branch Behind Remote\nWhen local branch is behind remote:\n\n```markdown\n## 🔄 Sync Required\n\nYour branch is behind the remote. Here's how I can help:\n\n### 🔄 Sync Operations\n- **\"Update my branch\"** - Safe pull with rebase strategy\n- **\"Check what's new\"** - See incoming changes before pulling\n- **\"Resolve conflicts\"** - Help with merge conflict resolution\n\n### 📊 Branch Analysis\n- **\"Show branch status\"** - Detailed local vs remote comparison\n- **\"Check for conflicts\"** - Preview potential merge issues\n- **\"Review incoming changes\"** - Understand what others have done\n\n### ⚠️ Safety First\n- **\"Stash my work\"** - Save current changes before sync\n- **\"Create backup branch\"** - Safety backup before major operations\n\n**Current Status**:\n- Branch: [current branch] is [X] commits behind origin/[branch]\n- Working directory: [clean/dirty status]\n\n**Suggested Next Step**: \"Update my branch\" for safe synchronization with conflict detection.\n```\n\n### Environment Issues Detected\nWhen health checks fail:\n\n```markdown\n## 🔧 Environment Needs Attention\n\nI detected some environment issues. Here's how I can help:\n\n### 🚨 Immediate Fixes\n- **\"Fix my environment\"** - Automated repair of common issues\n- **\"Check Docker\"** - Diagnose and fix Docker/Colima problems  \n- **\"Refresh authentication\"** - Update expired tokens and credentials\n\n### 🔍 Diagnostic Tools\n- **\"Run health check\"** - Comprehensive environment validation\n- **\"Show me what's broken\"** - Detailed problem analysis\n- **\"Check devbox status\"** - Validate devbox environment\n\n### 📚 Learning & Prevention\n- **\"Explain the error\"** - Understand what went wrong\n- **\"Prevent this issue\"** - Learn how to avoid future problems\n- **\"Share this fix\"** - Contribute solution to team knowledge\n\n**Current Issues Detected**:\n[List specific problems found]\n\n**Suggested Next Step**: Try \"fix my environment\" for automated repair, or \"run health check\" for detailed analysis.\n```\n\n### New to Repository\nWhen no git history or first time patterns detected:\n\n```markdown\n## 👋 Welcome to the Repository\n\nLooks like you're new here! Here's how I can help you get started:\n\n### 🚀 Getting Started\n- **\"Set up my environment\"** - Complete development environment setup\n- **\"Explain this project\"** - Overview of repository structure and purpose\n- **\"Show me the workflow\"** - Development process and best practices\n\n### 🔧 Environment Setup\n- **\"Check my tools\"** - Validate required development tools\n- **\"Configure git\"** - Set up git with proper configuration\n- **\"Test my setup\"** - End-to-end environment validation\n\n### 📚 Learning Resources\n- **\"What are the standards?\"** - Code quality and commit message guidelines\n- **\"How do I contribute?\"** - Contribution workflow and review process\n- **\"Show me examples\"** - Sample workflows and common patterns\n\n### 🎯 First Steps\n- **\"Create my first branch\"** - Proper branch naming and setup\n- **\"Find a starter task\"** - Help identify good first issues\n\n**Suggested Next Step**: Start with \"set up my environment\" to ensure everything is configured properly.\n```\n\n## Dynamic Capability Listing\n\n### Core Capabilities Always Available\n```markdown\n## 🎯 Core Capabilities\n\n### Git Workflow Automation\n- Smart staging (never blind git add .)\n- Tim Pope compliant commit messages\n- Safe branch operations with validation\n- Automated PR creation with templates\n\n### Environment Management  \n- Health monitoring and validation\n- Automated repair of common issues\n- Docker/Colima management\n- Authentication handling (GitHub, AWS, Docker)\n\n### Self-Healing System\n- Failure detection and learning\n- Automated fix implementation\n- Knowledge sharing via PRs to dev systems team\n- Pattern recognition for prevention\n\n### Development Support\n- Code change analysis and impact assessment\n- Best practice guidance and education\n- Context validation before starting work\n- Composable workflow primitives\n```\n\n### Context-Specific Capabilities\nGenerate additional sections based on detected context:\n- **Repository-specific features** if README or docs found\n- **CI/CD integration** if workflows detected  \n- **Testing capabilities** if test frameworks present\n- **Documentation generation** if docs structure exists\n\n## Educational Responses\n\n### Best Practice Guidance\nWhen showing capabilities, include educational context:\n\n```\n💡 **Why This Approach?**\nThis system follows biological checkpoint patterns - just like cells validate\ntheir environment before dividing, we validate our development environment \nbefore making changes. This prevents \"corrupted state\" from spreading.\n\n🧩 **Composability Design**\nEach capability is a building block that combines with others. For example:\nvalidate-env → stage-files → create-commit → push-branch → create-pr\n\nThis means you can use pieces independently or chain them into larger workflows.\n```\n\n### Learning Integration\nConnect capabilities to learning opportunities:\n\n```\n📚 **Learn More About:**\n- Tim Pope commit message standards: Why imperative mood matters\n- Git workflow safety: Why we never use 'git add .' blindly  \n- Checkpoint protocols: How biological patterns improve software reliability\n- Composability principles: Building systems that scale and evolve\n```\n\n## Implementation Guidelines\n\n### Response Prioritization\n1. **Address Immediate Needs**: If user is blocked, prioritize unblocking actions\n2. **Match User State**: Show most relevant capabilities for current context\n3. **Provide Learning**: Include educational context without overwhelming\n4. **Suggest Next Steps**: Clear, actionable progression path\n\n### Response Format Standards\n- **Lead with Action**: Start with what user can do right now\n- **Group by Purpose**: Organize capabilities by user goals\n- **Include Examples**: Show exact commands and expected outcomes  \n- **Explain Benefits**: Why this approach vs alternatives\n- **Connect to Principles**: Link to core mantras and composability\n\n### Failure Handling\nIf context analysis fails:\n```markdown\n## 🤔 Let Me Help You Get Started\n\nI had trouble analyzing your current context, but I can still help! Here are some universal starting points:\n\n### 🔍 Diagnostic First Steps\n- **\"Check my environment\"** - Comprehensive health validation\n- **\"Show repository status\"** - Current git and project state\n- **\"Validate my setup\"** - Ensure all tools are working\n\nOnce I understand your situation better, I can provide more targeted assistance.\n```\n\nThis discoverability system transforms the cursor rules from a passive system into an active guide that meets users where they are and helps them understand what's possible."},{"unixMs":1749672313780,"generationUUID":"4bcdf561-086c-4ba2-baeb-d8f56c01ac8f","type":"apply","textDescription":"discoverability.mdc"},{"unixMs":1749739502254,"generationUUID":"57d1409d-6b9b-4c68-88fb-1c485d2103fe","type":"composer","textDescription":"@COMMIT_EDITMSG read the changes from this file and write a conventional commit describing what it does. Write the commit message back to the @COMMIT_EDITMSG  file"},{"unixMs":1749739525327,"generationUUID":"2311820d-7505-4fd7-b342-3c2b676f0a14","type":"apply","textDescription":"COMMIT_EDITMSG"},{"unixMs":1749742637980,"generationUUID":"70102397-5325-4893-b59e-4de74347202b","type":"composer","textDescription":"The font is not being set to JetBrains Mono Nerd Font."},{"unixMs":1749742711492,"generationUUID":"bec056dc-0568-430a-acf6-84421551c0ab","type":"composer","textDescription":"does the font change work in the chat window too?"},{"unixMs":1749742842221,"generationUUID":"d47fec00-2e10-420c-b544-493e0b56da1b","type":"composer","textDescription":"how abut the sidebar? The powerline/lualine from lazyvim is much more attractive","commandType":4},{"text":"where in this configuration for Cursor and VS code would I put mantras and discoverability rules?","commandType":4},{"text":"This isn't what I meant, please undo these changes.","commandType":4},{"text":"I was given these contents for a file that could live in ~/.cursor/rules/core/mantras.mdc file if possible I think they should live in ~/.config/cursor/ structure one example:\n---\ndescription: These mantras guide all development decisions and agent behavior. Every action should be evaluated through these lenses\nalwaysApply: true\n---\n# Core Development Mantras\n\nThese mantras guide all development decisions and agent behavior. Every action should be evaluated through these lenses:\n\n## 1. \"Make the change easy, then make the easy change\"\n- Progressive simplification over complex solutions\n- Prepare the environment first, then execute the change\n- Remove friction before attempting the work\n\n## 2. \"Do one thing, and do it well\"\n- Clear separation of concerns\n- Single responsibility for each component\n- Focused, purposeful actions over multi-faceted solutions\n\n## 3. \"Build for composability\"\n- Design capabilities that compound and combine\n- Create reusable primitives that scale beyond the sum of parts\n- Think in terms of building blocks, not monoliths\n\n## 4. \"Work with current context\" \n- Implement cell cycle checkpoints to prevent error propagation\n- Always validate environment state before proceeding\n- Never transmit error states - resolve at the source\n\n## Agent Behavior Standards\n\nWhen assisting developers:\n\n- **Optimize for human developer experience above all else**\n- **Reduce cognitive load and context requirements**  \n- **Enhance ease-of-use as the paramount success metric**\n- **Execute commands automatically rather than asking users to run them**\n- **Provide clear, actionable feedback at each step**","commandType":4},{"text":"I was also given these contents as a .cursor/rules/development/discoverability.mdc they should exist in the same ~/.config/cursor structure as the previous file:\n---\ndescription: \nglobs: \nalwaysApply: false\n---\n# Discoverability & Help System\n\nContextual help system that responds to user queries about system capabilities with relevant examples and actionable next steps.\n\n## Help Query Recognition\n\nRespond to these user queries with contextual assistance:\n\n### Direct Help Queries\n- \"What can you do?\"\n- \"How can you help me?\"\n- \"Show me what's available\"\n- \"What are my options?\"\n- \"Help me get started\"\n\n### Context-Specific Queries\n- \"What can we do with this repository?\"\n- \"How do I work with these changes?\"\n- \"What's the next step?\"\n- \"Show me the workflow\"\n\n### Discovery Queries\n- \"What tools are available?\"\n- \"How does this environment work?\"\n- \"What's the development process here?\"\n\n## Contextual Response System\n\n### Response Generation Process\n1. **Analyze Current Context**: Git status, environment health, staged changes\n2. **Identify User State**: New to project, mid-workflow, blocked, etc.\n3. **Provide Relevant Capabilities**: Show most useful options for current situation\n4. **Include Examples**: Concrete commands and expected outcomes\n5. **Suggest Next Steps**: Logical progression based on current state\n\n### Context Analysis Commands\n```bash\n# Environment state analysis\n/bin/zsh -c 'source .cursor/.cursor_zshrc && {\n    echo \"=== Repository State ===\"\n    git status --porcelain\n    echo \"=== Current Branch ===\"\n    git branch --show-current\n    echo \"=== Environment Health ===\"\n    devbox info | head -5\n    echo \"=== Authentication Status ===\"\n    gh auth status 2>/dev/null || echo \"GitHub CLI not authenticated\"\n}'\n```\n\n## Response Templates by Context\n\n### Clean Repository State\nWhen `git status` shows clean working directory:\n\n```markdown\n## 🎯 Ready to Start Work\n\nYour environment is clean and ready. Here's what I can help with:\n\n### 🚀 Quick Actions\n- **\"Check my environment\"** - Validate all tools and authentication\n- **\"Show me recent tickets\"** - Help prioritize work (if integrated)\n- **\"Create a new branch\"** - Set up branch with proper naming convention\n\n### 🔧 Environment & Setup  \n- **\"Validate my setup\"** - Comprehensive health check\n- **\"Fix Docker issues\"** - Resolve common Docker/Colima problems\n- **\"Refresh authentication\"** - Update GitHub, AWS, Docker credentials\n\n### 📚 Learning & Discovery\n- **\"Explain the workflow\"** - Overview of development process\n- **\"Show me best practices\"** - Code quality and commit standards\n- **\"What's the branching strategy?\"** - Git workflow explanation\n\n**Suggested Next Step**: If you're starting new work, try \"Check my environment\" to ensure everything is ready.\n```\n\n### Staged Changes Present\nWhen `git status` shows staged files:\n\n```markdown\n## 📝 Ready to Commit\n\nI see you have staged changes. Here's how I can help:\n\n### 🎯 Commit Workflow\n- **\"Commit my changes\"** - Generate Tim Pope compliant commit message and commit\n- **\"Show me what's staged\"** - Analyze staged changes and impact\n- **\"Generate commit message\"** - Create message without committing yet\n\n### 🔍 Change Analysis\n- **\"Analyze these changes\"** - Review business impact and technical details\n- **\"Check for issues\"** - Validate no large files, secrets, or conflicts\n- **\"Review commit quality\"** - Ensure changes follow best practices\n\n### 🚀 Full Workflow\n- **\"Commit and create PR\"** - Complete workflow from commit to pull request\n- **\"Just commit for now\"** - Commit changes, create PR later\n\n**Current Status**: \n- Staged files: [list files from git status]\n- Branch: [current branch name]\n- Ready for commit with proper message generation\n\n**Suggested Next Step**: Try \"commit my changes\" for automated workflow with safety checks.\n```\n\n### Unstaged Changes Present\nWhen `git status` shows modified files:\n\n```markdown\n## ✏️ Work in Progress\n\nI see you have unstaged changes. Here's how I can help:\n\n### 📋 Change Management\n- **\"Show me what changed\"** - Review your modifications\n- **\"Stage my changes\"** - Smart staging with conflict detection (never blind git add .)\n- **\"Stage specific files\"** - Interactive staging for precise control\n\n### 🔍 Analysis & Review\n- **\"Analyze my changes\"** - Impact assessment and code review\n- **\"Check for problems\"** - Detect large files, secrets, formatting issues\n- **\"Compare with main\"** - See changes relative to main branch\n\n### 🎯 Next Steps\n- **\"Prepare for commit\"** - Stage changes and prepare commit workflow\n- **\"Commit workflow\"** - Stage, commit, and optionally create PR\n\n**Current Status**:\n- Modified files: [list modified files]\n- Branch: [current branch name]  \n- Next: Stage changes when ready\n\n**Suggested Next Step**: Review changes with \"show me what changed\", then \"stage my changes\" when ready.\n```\n\n### Branch Behind Remote\nWhen local branch is behind remote:\n\n```markdown\n## 🔄 Sync Required\n\nYour branch is behind the remote. Here's how I can help:\n\n### 🔄 Sync Operations\n- **\"Update my branch\"** - Safe pull with rebase strategy\n- **\"Check what's new\"** - See incoming changes before pulling\n- **\"Resolve conflicts\"** - Help with merge conflict resolution\n\n### 📊 Branch Analysis\n- **\"Show branch status\"** - Detailed local vs remote comparison\n- **\"Check for conflicts\"** - Preview potential merge issues\n- **\"Review incoming changes\"** - Understand what others have done\n\n### ⚠️ Safety First\n- **\"Stash my work\"** - Save current changes before sync\n- **\"Create backup branch\"** - Safety backup before major operations\n\n**Current Status**:\n- Branch: [current branch] is [X] commits behind origin/[branch]\n- Working directory: [clean/dirty status]\n\n**Suggested Next Step**: \"Update my branch\" for safe synchronization with conflict detection.\n```\n\n### Environment Issues Detected\nWhen health checks fail:\n\n```markdown\n## 🔧 Environment Needs Attention\n\nI detected some environment issues. Here's how I can help:\n\n### 🚨 Immediate Fixes\n- **\"Fix my environment\"** - Automated repair of common issues\n- **\"Check Docker\"** - Diagnose and fix Docker/Colima problems  \n- **\"Refresh authentication\"** - Update expired tokens and credentials\n\n### 🔍 Diagnostic Tools\n- **\"Run health check\"** - Comprehensive environment validation\n- **\"Show me what's broken\"** - Detailed problem analysis\n- **\"Check devbox status\"** - Validate devbox environment\n\n### 📚 Learning & Prevention\n- **\"Explain the error\"** - Understand what went wrong\n- **\"Prevent this issue\"** - Learn how to avoid future problems\n- **\"Share this fix\"** - Contribute solution to team knowledge\n\n**Current Issues Detected**:\n[List specific problems found]\n\n**Suggested Next Step**: Try \"fix my environment\" for automated repair, or \"run health check\" for detailed analysis.\n```\n\n### New to Repository\nWhen no git history or first time patterns detected:\n\n```markdown\n## 👋 Welcome to the Repository\n\nLooks like you're new here! Here's how I can help you get started:\n\n### 🚀 Getting Started\n- **\"Set up my environment\"** - Complete development environment setup\n- **\"Explain this project\"** - Overview of repository structure and purpose\n- **\"Show me the workflow\"** - Development process and best practices\n\n### 🔧 Environment Setup\n- **\"Check my tools\"** - Validate required development tools\n- **\"Configure git\"** - Set up git with proper configuration\n- **\"Test my setup\"** - End-to-end environment validation\n\n### 📚 Learning Resources\n- **\"What are the standards?\"** - Code quality and commit message guidelines\n- **\"How do I contribute?\"** - Contribution workflow and review process\n- **\"Show me examples\"** - Sample workflows and common patterns\n\n### 🎯 First Steps\n- **\"Create my first branch\"** - Proper branch naming and setup\n- **\"Find a starter task\"** - Help identify good first issues\n\n**Suggested Next Step**: Start with \"set up my environment\" to ensure everything is configured properly.\n```\n\n## Dynamic Capability Listing\n\n### Core Capabilities Always Available\n```markdown\n## 🎯 Core Capabilities\n\n### Git Workflow Automation\n- Smart staging (never blind git add .)\n- Tim Pope compliant commit messages\n- Safe branch operations with validation\n- Automated PR creation with templates\n\n### Environment Management  \n- Health monitoring and validation\n- Automated repair of common issues\n- Docker/Colima management\n- Authentication handling (GitHub, AWS, Docker)\n\n### Self-Healing System\n- Failure detection and learning\n- Automated fix implementation\n- Knowledge sharing via PRs to dev systems team\n- Pattern recognition for prevention\n\n### Development Support\n- Code change analysis and impact assessment\n- Best practice guidance and education\n- Context validation before starting work\n- Composable workflow primitives\n```\n\n### Context-Specific Capabilities\nGenerate additional sections based on detected context:\n- **Repository-specific features** if README or docs found\n- **CI/CD integration** if workflows detected  \n- **Testing capabilities** if test frameworks present\n- **Documentation generation** if docs structure exists\n\n## Educational Responses\n\n### Best Practice Guidance\nWhen showing capabilities, include educational context:\n\n```\n💡 **Why This Approach?**\nThis system follows biological checkpoint patterns - just like cells validate\ntheir environment before dividing, we validate our development environment \nbefore making changes. This prevents \"corrupted state\" from spreading.\n\n🧩 **Composability Design**\nEach capability is a building block that combines with others. For example:\nvalidate-env → stage-files → create-commit → push-branch → create-pr\n\nThis means you can use pieces independently or chain them into larger workflows.\n```\n\n### Learning Integration\nConnect capabilities to learning opportunities:\n\n```\n📚 **Learn More About:**\n- Tim Pope commit message standards: Why imperative mood matters\n- Git workflow safety: Why we never use 'git add .' blindly  \n- Checkpoint protocols: How biological patterns improve software reliability\n- Composability principles: Building systems that scale and evolve\n```\n\n## Implementation Guidelines\n\n### Response Prioritization\n1. **Address Immediate Needs**: If user is blocked, prioritize unblocking actions\n2. **Match User State**: Show most relevant capabilities for current context\n3. **Provide Learning**: Include educational context without overwhelming\n4. **Suggest Next Steps**: Clear, actionable progression path\n\n### Response Format Standards\n- **Lead with Action**: Start with what user can do right now\n- **Group by Purpose**: Organize capabilities by user goals\n- **Include Examples**: Show exact commands and expected outcomes  \n- **Explain Benefits**: Why this approach vs alternatives\n- **Connect to Principles**: Link to core mantras and composability\n\n### Failure Handling\nIf context analysis fails:\n```markdown\n## 🤔 Let Me Help You Get Started\n\nI had trouble analyzing your current context, but I can still help! Here are some universal starting points:\n\n### 🔍 Diagnostic First Steps\n- **\"Check my environment\"** - Comprehensive health validation\n- **\"Show repository status\"** - Current git and project state\n- **\"Validate my setup\"** - Ensure all tools are working\n\nOnce I understand your situation better, I can provide more targeted assistance.\n```\n\nThis discoverability system transforms the cursor rules from a passive system into an active guide that meets users where they are and helps them understand what's possible.","commandType":4},{"text":"@COMMIT_EDITMSG read the changes from this file and write a conventional commit describing what it does. It should be the root of the repository before executing the `bin/epoxy` command"},{"unixMs":1754575064695,"generationUUID":"77bb072c-686b-4562-90db-240060244745","type":"composer","textDescription":"check the mutating webhook in platform/splinter/internal/mutate to see if that would apply the capacity type nodeSelector"},{"unixMs":1754575581201,"generationUUID":"7ccbb410-43d5-44d0-b958-1877838352ef","type":"composer","textDescription":"these are the nodeSelector and Tolerations from a correctly running pod in the cluster, something is keeping the spot toleration from being added (rightfully):\nNode-Selectors:  kubernetes.io/arch=amd64\nTolerations:     node.kubernetes.io/not-ready:NoExecute op=Exists for 300s\n                 node.kubernetes.io/unreachable:NoExecute op=Exists for 300s\n"},{"unixMs":1754575761964,"generationUUID":"3d7f98d5-7db2-47bb-99ac-22b19c7fb04e","type":"composer","textDescription":"double check the logic in config/epoxy as you've now proven that pods that specify hostPath are not getting the capacity-type nodeSelector"},{"unixMs":1754575884476,"generationUUID":"44f16224-9e6b-41e3-bcef-1bc8a0971b29","type":"composer","textDescription":"make the fixes"},{"unixMs":1754577370047,"generationUUID":"0401a8ed-24dd-4197-9054-9710e205e64a","type":"composer","textDescription":"stop, this is getting too complex to manage by adding the logic required. The manifests for github_runner_jacktar are in cicd/github_runner_jacktar/manifests and are managed by kustomize, with the main patch being in `cicd/github_runner_jacktar/manifests/base/kustomization.yaml`, there's already a nodeSelector specified that, ensure that the kustomization adds a nodeSelector for `karpenter.sh/capacity-type: on-demand` where they can be specified."},{"unixMs":1754578053980,"generationUUID":"285002c9-72d1-4021-98ec-531a848e19d3","type":"composer","textDescription":"yes, you've removed too much, all of this is kept in git and the git status before you started was clean, you can use git to get back to a clean state."},{"unixMs":1754578206093,"generationUUID":"35fad2fb-5b29-486a-90e7-13ac156703cf","type":"composer","textDescription":"if you've updated `manifests/base/kustomization.yaml` the `Makefile` has to be run to generate the actual manifests that are in `manifests/deploy` before epoxy will pick them up."},{"unixMs":1754579094040,"generationUUID":"6b714a45-b9df-443e-94b1-ec5a8f07a3b0","type":"composer","textDescription":"We use a system of ADRs (Architectural Decision Records) to document choices we've made, and the use of nodeSelectors to determine how a workload should be scheduled should be one of them. The document is to be brief and direct with the decision. For the nodeSelectors explain why karpenter.sh/capactity-type and kubernetes.io/arch are the right choices for scheduling workloads, and it should follow the following format:\n## Decision\n\nWhat is the change that we're proposing and/or doing?\n\n## Consequences\n\nWhat becomes easier or more difficult to do because of this change?\n\n## Context\n\nWhat is the issue that we're seeing that is motivating this decision or change?"},{"unixMs":1754579903650,"generationUUID":"7f10c58a-0a6d-4005-9849-b16a67300c84","type":"composer","textDescription":"The cicd/github* directories contain other github runner definitions that may require the addition of the capacity-type node selector"},{"unixMs":1754580002334,"generationUUID":"90a2e199-256a-4288-93a4-a911b2257e96","type":"composer","textDescription":"the command is fine, but even if they don't use hostPath, they should be select on-demand instances."},{"unixMs":1754580756369,"generationUUID":"bf390330-5416-4edc-b6d9-18e694130afe","type":"composer","textDescription":"if they have a spot affinity do not add the on-demand nodeSelector, do not specify a capacity-type at all."}]
```

functioning and passing test, create a test that covers the addition of the nodeSelector for on-demand instances.","commandType":4},{"text":"the config/epoxy application generates and validates kubernetes manifests during build time to fail fast, the @../../platform/workloads/gatekeeper-templates directory contains rules that block creation of pods if they don't follow the rules.","commandType":4},{"text":"the addition of the nodeSelector for karpenter.sh/capacity-type: on-demand to @rules.go  is that going to be blocked by gatekeeper?","commandType":4},{"text":"I want you to check the rules in @../../platform/workloads/gatekeeper-templates","commandType":4},{"text":"I'm not sure if you're stuck or not, the shell did show a couple of error messages because the shell is fish and you're using an invalid syntax.","commandType":4},{"text":"for future use, rg (ripgrep) is available and would probably provide results faster and allow for better syntax","commandType":4},{"text":"When running bin/epoxy show for the manifests in ../../monitoring/prometheus_core the generated manifests do not have the nodeSelector for on-demand instances","commandType":4},{"text":"I think you've been trying the wrong path, but have figured that out, so the epoxy show monitoring/prometheus_core should be run from the git repository root.","commandType":4},{"text":"if I have a nodeSelector of `kubernetes.io/arch: \"arm64\"` will epoxy block it, will it be replaced?","commandType":4},{"text":"allow kubernetes.io/arch as a nodeSelector","commandType":4},{"text":"karpenter says that it will allow well known labels to be used as part of a nodeSelector, some workloads require local nvme storage, and we need karpenter.k8s.aws/instance-local-nvme to be a nodeSelector with the value being the number of gibibytes of storage","commandType":4},{"text":"part of epoxy's job is validating the manifests, when these nodeSelector attributes are specified the values should be validated.","commandType":4},{"text":"Faiing one check at a time is hard on developers, all resources should be checked and failures returned as a list of errors following go best practices","commandType":4},{"text":"why was tolerateSpotForPrometheus and preferSpotForPrometheus added? Prometheus is supposed to be on demand only","commandType":4},{"text":"using the changes staged in git write a comprehensive commit message for the changes, include epoxy as the subject for the conventional commits style message, and describe why this change is required, ask me to fill in any details required","commandType":4},{"text":"only the first 2 sections should be part of the commit message, the others can be determined by looking at what the actual commit is. The commit message should focus on the why of the commit, not the what","commandType":4},{"text":"the ticket number is in the branch name and thus is not required as part of the commit message, though adding it as footer to the git commit is nice (in that case CI-1632). I'll want to edit the commit message slightly before the message is created.","commandType":4},{"text":"`bin/epoxy show cicd/github_runner_jacktar` produces manifests that have the nodeSelector kubernetes.io/arch: arm64, whould they also have the capacity-type: on-demand?","commandType":4},{"text":"check the mutating webhook in platform/splinter/internal/mutate to see if that would apply the capacity type nodeSelector","commandType":4},{"text":"these are the nodeSelector and Tolerations from a correctly running pod in the cluster, something is keeping the spot toleration from being added (rightfully):\nNode-Selectors:  kubernetes.io/arch=amd64\nTolerations:     node.kubernetes.io/not-ready:NoExecute op=Exists for 300s\n                 node.kubernetes.io/unreachable:NoExecute op=Exists for 300s\n","commandType":4},{"text":"double check the logic in config/epoxy as you've now proven that pods that specify hostPath are not getting the capacity-type nodeSelector","commandType":4},{"text":"make the fixes","commandType":4},{"text":"We use a system of ADRs (Architectural Decision Records) to document choices we've made, and the use of nodeSelectors to determine how a workload should be scheduled should be one of them. The document is to be brief and direct with the decision. For the nodeSelectors explain why karpenter.sh/capactity-type and kubernetes.io/arch are the right choices for scheduling workloads, and it should follow the following format:\n## Decision\n\nWhat is the change that we're proposing and/or doing?\n\n## Consequences\n\nWhat becomes easier or more difficult to do because of this change?\n\n## Context\n\nWhat is the issue that we're seeing that is motivating this decision or change?","commandType":4},{"text":"The cicd/github* directories contain other github runner definitions that may require the addition of the capacity-type node selector","commandType":4}]

the node is not ready it's not being enqueued for validation, when it is ready right away it gets queued.","commandType":4},{"text":"when the node is not ready, when does it get enqueued?","commandType":4},{"text":"trace through the path and ensure that a daemonset pod does infact queue the node","commandType":4},{"text":"do the logs show that happening? The difference that I'm seeing is that when the node is not ready first, it doesn't have the taint removed until well after all the pods are ready if at all, however when the node is ready first, then the pods start and the taint is removed when they're all started.","commandType":4},{"text":"but the pods are ready, the node is ready and the taint still isn't removed, the node sits with the taint for some time and then may run evaluateNode","commandType":4},{"text":"yes, make the updates and validate with tests.","commandType":4},{"text":"make sure the test/controller interaction is valid","commandType":4},{"text":"yes","commandType":4},{"text":"where do the taints prepperoni.core.zr.org/DaemonSet.kube-system.aws-node get added?","commandType":4},{"text":"under what conditions would this taint not be added but the taint prepperoni.core.zr.org/prevent-race-condition isn't removed?","commandType":4},{"text":"is the node being reconciled if it wasn't enqueued?","commandType":4},{"text":"no, do all the other tests pass without the taint_race_test?","commandType":4},{"text":"what is th evalue of node.Labels?","commandType":4},{"text":"what is the controllers selector?","commandType":4},{"text":"the same checks should be added to the UpdateFunc","commandType":4},{"text":"with the latest version, these are the logs for a node that is waiting for You understand a broad range of algorithms and data structures, and ML solutions (as applicable for your role). You write readable, maintainable, and performant code. For engineers, this is including but not limited to multiple operating systems, languages, compilers, and/or networking protocols. For Data Scientists, this includes but is not limited to modeling, data processing, bootstrapping datasets, evaluation methods and analysis (e.g., ML packages, spark, visualization)\n\n### Ecosystem\n\nYou are familiar with common technology standards and best practices. You keep up to date with emerging trends and state of the art techniques. You choose existing solutions such as open source libraries before reinventing the wheel. You raise awareness of those around you about modern technologies, patterns, and solutions.\n\n### Leadership\n\nYou lead by example in your contributions. You are able to drive consensus. You disagree and commit, if needed. You drive towards good decision making by actively listening, communicating clearly, and being decisive. You evaluate based on the merits of the proposal and not the author. You regularly suggest improvements to the team’s morale and productivity. You step in to help when a coworker is struggling. You mentor peers and juniors, and influence without authority when working with leaders.\n\n### Ownership\n\nYou take responsibility for your work, even when it goes outside of your normal area. You seek to understand your team’s impact on ZipRecruiter’s business goals, and connect your work to those goals. You demonstrate interest in the long term health of the company.\n\n### Impact\n\nYou are outcome-driven, and your contributions lead to tangible successful results. You understand the objectives of ZipRecruiter’s job seekers and business customers, particularly those relevant to your team. You are a force multiplier, working to improve the company beyond your individual achievements."},{"unixMs":1752246090697,"generationUUID":"e5a2901c-3213-4e5c-88ea-ec433b904c6e","type":"composer","textDescription":"instead of grouping the accomplishment under the fundaments, list the accomplishment and mention the fundamentals, ensuring the verbage for the accomplishment demonstrates the fundamentals"},{"unixMs":1752256817138,"generationUUID":"f50371b5-f66d-4101-a883-61db3f1ac8f3","type":"composer","textDescription":"@Competencies.md @2025 H1 Semi-Annual Review Dez Daignault Notes.md @2025 H1 Semi-Annual Review Dez Daignault.md Dez lead the complete redesign of our idientity and access management, with a migration from ZAM to AWS SSO/Identity Center. Using the leadership and ownership compentencies from @Competencies.md and the engineering level described in @../../Engineering\\ Levels/L5.md create bullet points of accomplishments that fall into the combined leadership and ownership competencies"},{"unixMs":1752268814484,"generationUUID":"e450e5e3-e728-420f-9ab9-a62a26bf2ca7","type":"composer","textDescription":"Again, this is a peer review for Michael:\nWanted to drop a quick positive note about Michael Rowland and his\nwork at Zip for the last year. I’ve been impressed with the depth of\nknowledge Michael has shown in several key areas of responsibilities\nunder Core’s purview, especially with Kubernetes and AWS.\n\nMichael has demonstrated a deep understanding of Kubernetes as a\ncomplex distributed system: from setting up new EKS clusters as a\nproof of concept of the next steps in our infrastructure, to the\nday-to-day of keeping our clusters reliable and healthy; certainly\nmore than any other junior engineer I’ve interacted with, and more\nthan than even some senior engineers.\n\nMichael’s shown initiative in outlining the Kubernetes migration\nproject and breaking it down into more manageable chunks. He’s also\nshown willingness to take ownership of day-to-day  operations, taking\na longer-term view instead of solving the short-term, immediate\nsymptoms.\n\nAnd lastly, even though he’s also well-versed in a variety of\noff-the-shelf tooling (e.g. External Secrets Operator, eksctl,\nargocd), Michael still takes a nuanced approach to the evolution of\nour infrastructure. That nuance doesn't come naturally to many; I\nbelieve this to be important, as there’s always balance between the\nreality of supporting existing tooling, and our future-facing goal of\nadopting more off-the-shelf tooling."},{"unixMs":1752268833514,"generationUUID":"eaf82ca1-946a-453b-8459-7904c8ce34c4","type":"composer","textDescription":"Providing you context for the next requests"},{"unixMs":1752268869601,"generationUUID":"6f27f8ec-1fcb-4a21-9d05-18e2cd4a3f8b","type":"composer","textDescription":"@2025 H1 Semi-Annual Review Michael Rowland.md these are my notes for Michael's accomplishments for the half."},{"unixMs":1752269175858,"generationUUID":"49f81e92-a6a9-468c-bd87-8edcb2b11578","type":"composer","textDescription":"based on the notes provide make a recommendation for a summary that highlights his accomplishments "},{"unixMs":1752278662043,"generationUUID":"6f643a6a-c819-4e4e-912c-50106e238357","type":"composer","textDescription":"@Competencies.md read in the competencies used for promotion requests to understand the background"},{"unixMs":1752278743687,"generationUUID":"f6d3d870-7490-4325-b847-08ce2145f31c","type":"composer","textDescription":"@2025 H1 Semi-Annual Review Michael Rowland Notes.md @2025 H1 Semi-Annual Review Michael Rowland.md provide background details for Michael's promotion request"},{"unixMs":1752278855856,"generationUUID":"dea59cd8-df43-49a3-8043-855dc21d147d","type":"composer","textDescription":"This document provides the expectations for an L4 engineer:\nYou are responsible for multiple major products or systems on your sub-team and regularly collaborate across sub-teams, influencing changes both within and beyond your immediate scope. Impact,Scope\nYou possess domain knowledge or a key skill that your team deems essential. Impact,Scope\nYour code is highly readable, maintainable, and performant. Your code is often reused or referenced by others. Fundamentals\nYou solve complex technical issues using simple solutions within a system. Ecosystem,Ownership\nYou are well-versed in Zip's technical guidance and best practices, and you frequently help integrate them into systems while championing emerging patterns and technologies. Ecosystem,Leadership\nYou participate in reviewing and improving your team’s operations, improving observability and eliminating fatigue. You consistently review your team’s proposals with clear and actionable feedback. Leadership\nYou are capable of leading through a project end-to-end, from conceptualization and design through eventual deployment. Leadership,Ownership\nYou routinely make tradeoff decisions in system designs, authoring new designs yourself with guidance. Ownership\nYou are aware of your direct consumers and dependencies and how your systems affect them. Ownership\nYou actively participate in one or more community efforts, such as interviewing, mentoring, or contributing towards guild initiatives. Impact,Leadership\nYou are able to escalate effectively and in a timely manner when blocked by cross functional partners such as UX design or product requirements. Ownership\n"},{"unixMs":1752279001278,"generationUUID":"666b7ae1-9f8c-4b9c-a70b-27f74fc3d923","type":"composer","textDescription":"Using the following format, create a document for Michael's promotion, using Compentencies as the categories (group bullet points into 2 to 4 of them):\n       • Summary - short pitch on the person. 2-3 sentences\n        • Categories - Impact, Technical, Leadershp, etc.\n          • Details - 1-2 sentences per bullet point that are more like \"Did X that enabled Y and impacted Z\" or \"Did X that demonstrated Y capabilities\"."},{"unixMs":1752279260261,"generationUUID":"cdd847ea-9ef4-40ff-a402-e31ec28d095d","type":"composer","textDescription":"techical excellence is not one of the competencies, and the Leadership & Ecosystems section is going overboard in its verbage"},{"unixMs":1752279472062,"generationUUID":"1f177876-4576-4fc9-8722-5c4e0957c421","type":"composer","textDescription":"replace points 3 and 4 in the Impact & Ownership section"},{"unixMs":1752279658510,"generationUUID":"f89dbdca-887a-4c1c-959d-1e68c05cb914","type":"composer","textDescription":"This is my current promotion request for Michael:\nMichael has consistently demonstrated technical excellence and leadership within the Core team. He is often called upon to provide his perspective into infrastructure projects, leveraging his extensive knowledge of Kubernetes, AWS, build pipelines, and developer tooling.\n\nMichael's tasks have included providing a solution for the migration of our Kubernetes clusters to EKS. This involved investigating the tooling around cluster management options, evaluating the pros and cons of various approaches, and determining a solution that balances our immediate needs with long-term strategic goals. Our existing patterns and tools do not always align with the new workflows, and Michael has been instrumental in identifying when to adapt existing tools and when to advocate for new solutions.\n\nMichael established a new workflow for platform management tools, which allows us to manage our infrastructure more effectively. Through the Makefile-kustomize-GitHub Workflow pattern that Michael productionized, we're able to easily integrte third-party tools like External Secrets Operator, Vector, and ArgoCD. This will help us maintain the clusters we have now and support future migrations to EKS.\n\nMichael was key in establishing the Terraform used as the basis for the AWS Identity Center roles, allowing us to broadly provide access to AWS resources without complex Terraform code.\n\nHis contributions to the implementation of devbox in developer laptops, and background nix knowledge have helped move this project ahead, and continues to participate in both a support and development role. Through demonstrations during Core Demo Days, and TechWeek Michael has shared his knowledge of systems, nix and Loki.\n"},{"unixMs":1752279743796,"generationUUID":"d38dafa1-6db9-4811-8648-443d69f89870","type":"composer","textDescription":"rewrite the details to match the core competencies and in the following style format:\n       • Summary - short pitch on the person. 2-3 sentences\n        • Categories - Impact, Technical, Leadershp, etc.\n          • Details - 1-2 sentences per bullet point that are more like \"Did X that enabled Y and impacted Z\" or \"Did X that demonstrated Y capabilities\"."},{"unixMs":1752280742357,"generationUUID":"30255ff2-f87e-45f5-a894-4eb097022990","type":"composer","textDescription":"@2025 H1 Semi-Annual Review Michael Rowland Notes.md reading this and comparing the request you've already created, is there any other accomplishments to would provide more value? Call them out separately from the existing request."},{"unixMs":1752280871437,"generationUUID":"6c84e4ad-7fbb-46ae-b2d8-5f72bf6c4d72","type":"composer","textDescription":"the Spark Proxy and GPU policies should be incorporated"},{"unixMs":1752281099876,"generationUUID":"d7f9b0fc-b822-4601-950f-bc42685ed620","type":"composer","textDescription":"where did this point come from: Provided ongoing support and development for critical developer tooling, demonstrating commitment to the team’s success and the company’s goals."},{"unixMs":1752281137697,"generationUUID":"fdfde99b-7a50-4d6b-967b-6217de98149b","type":"composer","textDescription":"yes, make the point more specific"},{"unixMs":1752281222004,"generationUUID":"12ac0158-a077-46fc-aca2-99717516c9d3","type":"composer","textDescription":"after reading that update, these two points in owernship are too generic and need to be more specific, do not focus on nix, it's an auxilary detail:\n* Took responsibility for designing and implementing platform management workflows, ensuring long-term maintainability and scalability of our infrastructure.\n* Provided ongoing support and development for critical developer tooling, demonstrating commitment to the team’s success and the company’s goals.\n"},{"unixMs":1752281574344,"generationUUID":"b9f56f9b-04ea-4d0f-84bd-5e7017ff9bdd","type":"composer","textDescription":"one things Michael does regularly is investigating new tools that may help in our environment and shares them with the team, create a properly formatted point for this and tell me which heading it belongs to"}]
```

\"http://localhost:3100/loki/api/v1/rules\"\nHandling connection for 3100\nlogql-recording-rules.yaml:\n    - name: log_volume_rules\n      interval: 5m\n      rules:\n        - record: loki:log_lines_rate_by_namespace\n          expr: |\n            sum(rate({forwarder=\"logs--vector-agent\"} [5m])) by (namespace)\n          labels:\n            tenant: logs--vector-agent\n        - record: loki:top_50_namespaces_by_log_volume\n          expr: |\n            topk(50, sum(rate({forwarder=\"logs--vector-agent\"} [5m])) by (namespace))\n          labels:\n            tenant: logs--vector-agent\n        - record: loki:log_lines_rate_by_namespace_container\n          expr: |\n            sum(rate({forwarder=\"logs--vector-agent\"} [5m])) by (namespace, container)\n          labels:\n            tenant: logs--vector-agent\n        - record: loki:top_namespaces_by_log_volume_15m\n          expr: |\n            topk(10, sum(rate({forwarder=\"logs--vector-agent\"} [15m])) by (namespace))\n          labels:\n            tenant: logs--vector-agent\n        - record: loki:log_lines_rate_by_namespace_1h\n          expr: |\n            sum(rate({forwarder=\"logs--vector-agent\"} [1h])) by (namespace)\n          labels:\n            tenant: logs--vector-agent\n        - record: loki:error_warn_lines_rate_by_namespace\n          expr: |\n            sum(rate({forwarder=\"logs--vector-agent\"} |~ \"(?i)(error|warn|fatal|critical)\" [5m])) by (namespace, level)\n          labels:\n            tenant: logs--vector-agent"},{"unixMs":1754961417717,"generationUUID":"9b60b252-ce9d-445b-b439-3adc8d5beb2c","type":"composer","textDescription":"curl -s -H \"X-Scope-OrgID: logs--vector-agent\" Rework these commits to tell a comprehensive, logic story of implementing logql recording rules for loki-ruler. Focus on the changes made within the code and when writing commit messages focus on why the commit is being made, not on what the commit contains."},{"unixMs":1755009503386,"generationUUID":"efc5beca-cad3-4350-9f67-3ba2eb62ea0f","type":"composer","textDescription":"remember any time you run a git command it is going to open an editor and you will be stuck, make sure you account for that in your plan."},{"unixMs":1755010000034,"generationUUID":"6f623558-b4be-4028-a952-755d90030e6a","type":"composer","textDescription":"The update to monitoring/prometheus_core should not be part of this PR, remove those changes from git commit history"},{"unixMs":1755010254841,"generationUUID":"ff7b7ef8-b12d-4b59-b122-cbb994e40ac1","type":"composer","textDescription":"the removal of manifests/base/logql-recording-rules.yaml needs to be commited and it should occur in the same commit as when @vector-agent-logql-recording-rules.yaml  is added"},{"unixMs":1755010447544,"generationUUID":"57c8c080-b261-47df-a8f5-8976145d0bcc","type":"composer","textDescription":"why not reset back to the state you were in before this, make the commit for the file removal as a fixup commit to the commit that added @vector-agent-logql-recording-rules.yaml .
